title: Investigate batch/amortized synchronization (batch-consume + batch-publish) for tail-latency reduction
description: Investigate amortizing per-message synchronization cost over BATCHES to reduce contention-driven fat tails in the latency histogram. Core hypothesis (user): batching may slightly RAISE median latency but LOWER p99.9 by collapsing N synchronizing operations (atomic acquire-load + acquire fence + cursor store, per message) into 1 per batch — N-times less coherence traffic on the shared cursor/published-sequence cache line. This directly serves the 'excellent, well-bounded latency' goal if the tail tightens. Two mechanisms in scope: (1) BATCH-CONSUME: consumer does one acquire-load of produced-seq, reads the whole available run with PLAIN non-atomic loads, commits its cursor ONCE. (2) BATCH-PUBLISH: producer writes N payloads then publishes with one release. The visibility-model fork (keep per-slot seqlock stamps vs add a shared published-counter for batch-publish) is itself to be investigated and reported, NOT pre-decided. Default batching policy = ADAPTIVE/opportunistic (take what's already published, never WAIT to fill a batch) so low-load latency is neutral; but measure the latency-vs-throughput-vs-tail tradeoff with data, don't assert it. Work on branch investigate/batch-amortized-sync. Builds on closed phase-1 (shimmy-4d4 ring, shimmy-7d8 harness).
design:
BATCH-CONSUME (prototyped, branch investigate/batch-amortized-sync). Consumer::read_batch() returns Consumer::Batch over [next_, produced()): ADAPTIVE/opportunistic, never waits to fill. Amortizes the two CROSS-CORE coherence ops over the run: ONE produced() acquire + ONE commit (consumer-cursor store) per batch. KEEPS per-slot seqlock validate() per-message.

CORRECTNESS DECISION (the hard part): validate() MUST stay per-message under Overwrite; it canNOT be hoisted to a once-per-batch boundary re-check. The seqlock version counter is PER-SLOT (each Slot has its own atomic seq). A run maps to many distinct slots; a consumer can be lapped on a MIDDLE slot while boundary slots are momentarily untouched, so a boundary-only re-check would certify a torn interior message. One validation per version counter => one validate per slot. This is the RIGHT split: validate touches the slot's OWN (already-resident) cache line; the two amortized ops are the contended cross-core lines. Verified TSan-clean by BatchConsume.BatchZeroCopyUnderMaximalLapping (2-slot ring, 300k msgs, constant lapping, zero torn) across repeated runs. Backpressure has no overwrite race => validate never fires, batch trivially safe, commit-amortization is pure win.

BATCH-PUBLISH FORK RECOMMENDATION: KEEP per-slot stamps; do NOT replace with a shared monotonic counter. Lap-detection (do_read empty/writing/lapped/ok) is derived purely from the per-slot stamp; a shared counter cannot tell a consumer whether ITS slot was lapped, so it breaks zero-copy validate(). A shared counter could only COEXIST (extra signal), not replace, and the per-slot stamp stores stay required for correctness => shared counter = cost without removing the stores. On x86-64 TSO the per-slot release stamps are already plain MOVs (cheap); the only genuinely-shared batch-publish saving is collapsing N Futex wakeups into 1 (a syscall-count win, not coherence). Right batch-publish shape: write N payloads, store N per-slot stamps (keep), one wake + one next_publish_ advance. Pursue only as a Futex-tier follow-up.

Full report: docs/batch-sync-findings.md (+ raw CSVs in docs/batch-data/).
acceptance_criteria: (1) A written findings report (in the bead --notes/--design and/or a docs file) covering: batch-consume design, batch-publish design, the per-slot-stamp-vs-shared-counter visibility fork with a data-backed recommendation, and correctness analysis for the Overwrite seqlock under batching. (2) A working PROTOTYPE of batch-consume (at minimum) on branch investigate/batch-amortized-sync, building clean under clang AND gcc, passing existing tests + new batch correctness tests, TSan-clean. (3) Benchmarked on the existing harness: batch-consume vs current per-message path, reporting MEASURED throughput delta AND p50/p99/p99.9 deltas (the tail hypothesis explicitly tested). (4) A clear recommendation: does batching reduce the tail? by how much? at what median cost? should batch-publish and/or bulk-reserve be pursued as follow-up beads? (5) All numbers from actual runs (noisy-config caveats stamped, per shimmy-7d8 harness). No speculation — data only.
notes:
MEASURED RESULTS (clang-18 Release, nix develop, AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370, 24 logical CPU, powersave+turbo => noisy=1; p50/p99 trustworthy, p99.9 noise-dominated, swings 10-100x run-to-run in BOTH modes; trustworthy signal = batch-vs-permsg delta same-box-same-run + stable median).

HYPOTHESIS VERDICT: partially confirmed + refined.
(1) MEDIAN RISE: CONFIRMED & STABLE. Batch raises p50 by ~+40-60ns on spin tiers at LIGHT load (gap=1000, batches~size1) — the extra produced() snapshot + bookkeeping. Futex ~0 (wakeup-dominated).
(2) TAIL-DOWN at LIGHT load: NOT cleanly observable here. permsg p99.9 seen 0.24us..110us for the SAME config across runs; batch sometimes wins big, sometimes loses. Cannot claim a light-load tail win on this noisy box. Needs quiet pinned host.
(3) TAIL at HEAVY load (gap=0): CLEAREST mechanistic confirmation. permsg p99.9 blows out and swings violently (~9us..305us); batch p99.9 is tight & STABLE (~14-23us). That is the contention-collapse predicted. CAVEAT: batch p50 rising to ~11us under gap=0 is mostly a QUEUEING ARTIFACT (message waits while batch drains the backlog ahead of it = queueing, not publish->visible) — so heavy-load p50 OVERSTATES the median cost; real median cost is the stable +40-60ns. Batch's heavy-load value is tail STABILITY, not median.
(4) THROUGHPUT (Overwrite, batch DRAIN vs permsg): CLEAN REPRODUCIBLE NO. Batch drain LOWERS producer publish rate: 64B/1c 155M->112M (-28..33%), 64B/2c 92M->62M (-33%), 256B/1c 95M->62M (-31%), 4KB/1c -6%. Reason: Overwrite consumers do NOT publish a cursor => NO commit store to amortize; batch only replaces N produced()-loads with 1 and its snapshot-then-drain churns resync() and perturbs next_publish_ MORE under lapping. Amortization target (cursor store) absent on this path.

REFINED MODEL: the 'shared cursor line' is TWO lines with opposite economics. Consumer-cursor STORE (Backpressure latency) = genuinely contended, amortize=win. Producer next_publish_ LOAD (Overwrite throughput) = consumer only reads, producer owns => amortize doesn't relieve producer, costs throughput. => batch-consume is a LATENCY/TAIL tool for the Backpressure/cursor-store path, NOT a throughput tool for Overwrite.

VERIFICATION: clang 32/32 ctest, gcc batch 6/6, TSan 31/31 (fork test compiled out) incl. repeated maximal-lapping batch runs, all clean under -Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic -Wconversion -Wshadow. Per-message path untouched (additive) => DESIGN 5 headline 155M @64B/1c still MET.

FILES: include/shimmy/ring.hpp (Consumer::Batch + read_batch), tests/ring_batch_test.cpp (6 tests) + tests/CMakeLists.txt, bench/latency_bench.cpp (Mode permsg|batch x 2 load levels, +mode,gap_ns CSV cols), bench/throughput_bench.cpp (Drain permsg|batch, +mode CSV col), docs/batch-sync-findings.md, docs/batch-data/*.csv.

FOLLOW-UPS: (a) quiet-host authoritative-tail re-measure (light-load tail-down still unconfirmed) — fold into shimmy-cvs. (b) batch-publish Futex-tier follow-up (keep per-slot stamps, collapse N wakeups->1, NO shared counter). (c) productionize batch-consume as opt-in Backpressure latency tool, keep OFF Overwrite throughput default.
close_reason: Investigation complete, hypothesis CLEANLY MEASURED and partially confirmed + refined (a measured NO on parts is as valuable as a YES). FINDINGS: (1) Median rise CONFIRMED & STABLE (+40-60ns spin tiers, light load) — the predicted small median cost. (2) Light-load tail-down NOT cleanly observable on this noisy box (p99.9 swings 10-100x in both modes) — stated as unconfirmed, needs quiet host (shimmy-cvs). (3) Heavy-load (gap=0) batch p99.9 is tight & STABLE (~14-23us) vs the violent per-message tail (~9-305us) = the contention-collapse the hypothesis predicts, clearest mechanistic evidence. (4) Overwrite-throughput batch drain is a CLEAN REPRODUCIBLE NO: -28..34% @64B, because Overwrite consumers publish no cursor so there is no commit store to amortize. REFINED MODEL: batch-consume is a LATENCY/TAIL tool for the Backpressure/cursor-store path, NOT a throughput tool for Overwrite. CORRECTNESS: validate() must stay per-message (per-slot version counters; a middle slot can be lapped while boundaries are untouched) — TSan-verified under maximal lapping. BATCH-PUBLISH FORK: keep per-slot stamps, reject shared counter (breaks lap-detection, can't eliminate the stamp stores); only Futex wakeup-collapse is worth pursuing. Prototype (additive, per-message API intact) + 6 tests + harness mode flags delivered on branch investigate/batch-amortized-sync; clang+gcc+TSan all clean; report in docs/batch-sync-findings.md. Follow-ups: shimmy-qui, shimmy-o93, shimmy-cvs comment. Not committed/pushed per instructions — main session integrates.

comments.author: Dustin
comments.text:
INITIAL DESIGN ANALYSIS (pre-code, reasoned not yet measured).

BATCH-CONSUME read-path: want=next; avail=produced.load(acquire); then iterate seq in [want,avail). The ONE acquire load of produced() replaces N per-slot acquire loads; the ONE commit(avail) (cursor store) replaces N cursor stores. That is the coherence-traffic saving the hypothesis predicts.

CRITICAL CORRECTNESS DECISION — validate() must stay PER-SLOT under Overwrite, NOT once-per-batch. Reasoning: each slot carries its OWN independent seqlock stamp (the version). A boundary re-check (re-validating only slot[want] and slot[avail-1]) canNOT certify the slots in the MIDDLE of the run: the producer can lap a middle slot while leaving the boundary slots momentarily untouched, so a single/boundary validate would commit torn middle messages. The seqlock contract is fundamentally per-version-counter; there is one counter per slot, so there must be one validate per slot consumed. Therefore batch-consume amortizes the PRODUCED-cursor acquire + the consumer-cursor commit (the SHARED, cross-core, ping-ponging cache lines), but keeps the per-slot stamp acquire-load + per-slot validate fence (the slot's OWN cache line, which the consumer is reading anyway — far cheaper, often already-resident). This is exactly the right split: we drop the cross-core coherence ops, keep the local per-slot ops. Under BACKPRESSURE there is no overwrite race at all (producer waits for us), so validate is a no-op there and the batch is trivially safe.

Plan: prototype adaptive/opportunistic batch-consume (take what produced() already shows, never wait to fill). Per-slot do_read+validate inside the batch, single produced() acquire at batch start, single commit at batch end. Mirror the ZeroCopyInPlaceUnderMaximalLapping stress for the batch path. Measure p50/p99/p99.9 under light AND heavy load.

(2) working batch-consume prototype on this branch, clang+gcc clean, 32/32 ctest, 6 new batch tests, TSan-clean incl. maximal-lapping batch stress. comments.author: Dustin
comments.text:
ARCHITECTURE ASSESSMENT — is validate() pluggable (concept/policy)? (assessment only, no code this round)

CURRENT STATE: validate() is a HARD-CODED member fn (ring.hpp:511), NOT a concept/policy. The file is internally inconsistent on pluggability — Wait strategy = concept-like (tag + static iface + is_wait_strategy trait, dispatched via template param + 'if constexpr (Wait::blocks)'); Overflow = tag type + is_overflow_policy_v ('if constexpr (is_same_v<Overflow,Backpressure>)'); Validation = NO abstraction. The Batch::validate() (ring.hpp:613) just delegates to Consumer::validate(), so it adds no flexibility. Patterns to imitate already exist in-file (wait strategy is the closest template for a Validator concept).

DEEP COUPLING IS THE DATA LAYOUT, NOT THE FUNCTION: validate() depends on (1) ring_->slot_at(read_seq_), (2) slot.seq = the PER-SLOT atomic<u64> version stamp (ring.hpp:121), (3) the acquire-fence + relaxed-reload seqlock algo. Any ALTERNATIVE validator (CRC/epoch/etc.) needing different per-slot state would require changing Slot's layout, not just Consumer. So validate() and Slot layout are ONE unit. A concept abstracting only the check (not the slot metadata) would be a leaky abstraction. This also touches the shmem header layout (shimmy-52e) which versions the slot layout.

A Validator concept (minimal, mirrors wait-strategy): template<typename V, typename Slot> concept Validator = requires(const Slot& s, u64 seq){ {V::check(s,seq)} -> same_as<bool>; }; — LOW COST if it only abstracts the check over the EXISTING Slot layout (seqlock vs no-op): two structs, a trait, one template param threaded through Ring+Consumer, ~half a day, if constexpr keeps the hot path identical. HIGH COST if validators need DIFFERENT slot state (CRC checksum field, epoch counter width): then Slot itself must be parameterized, shmem header (shimmy-52e) must version it, bench/test matrix multiplies — stops being a tidy concept, becomes a structural axis.

RECOMMENDATION (two tiers):
(1) WORTH DOING when a small win is wanted — specialize validation BY POLICY: shimmy-68t PROVED Backpressure never tears (producer never overwrites an unread slot), so its validate() is pure overhead (a fence + atomic reload that ALWAYS returns true). An internal 'if constexpr (is_same_v<Overflow,Backpressure>) return true;' (or a NoOpValidator tag) elides it. Low risk, mirrors existing if constexpr policy dispatch, removes a provably-unnecessary fence on the Backpressure read hot path. NOT a public concept — an internal specialization driven by a finding we already have. Candidate to fold into shimmy-o93 (productionize batch-consume as Backpressure latency tool) since both target the Backpressure read path.
(2) DEFER until data demands it — a PUBLIC user-extensible Validator concept. Feasible (wait strategies show the pattern) but the binding constraint is Slot layout, and NOTHING today justifies the cost. Zero data that the seqlock stamp is insufficient or that an alternative is better. Building a plugin surface for validators nobody has asked to write violates 'speculation is useless'. If a real non-seqlock validation need arises, THAT investigation (with a bench) justifies the abstraction — not before.

ONE-LINER: the architecture is READY for it (wait-strategy pattern is the template), but only the policy-specialization tier is JUSTIFIED by current data; the user-extensible concept is a solution in search of a measured problem.

title: Bootstrap: Nix devshell + build skeleton
description: Stand up the project skeleton so all subsequent work builds green. flake.nix Nix devshell with pinned toolchain (clang+gcc, C++20), CMake (or Meson) build, directory layout (include/shimmy/, bench/, tests/), HdrHistogram + a microbench dependency wired in, and a trivial stub that compiles and runs under 'nix develop'. This is the first claimed bead of the epic.
design: Nix flake for reproducible toolchain. CMake preferred unless Meson proves cleaner for header-only+bench layout. Layout: include/shimmy/ (header-only lib), bench/ (compiled, HdrHistogram, perf counters, CSV out), tests/ (correctness + TSan). Pin deps in flake for reproducible benchmark numbers — 'data doesn't lie' requires a fixed toolchain.
acceptance_criteria: 'nix develop' drops into a shell with C++20 clang+gcc, cmake, hdrhistogram available. A stub target compiles and runs. Directory layout (include/shimmy, bench, tests) exists. CI can invoke the build. Documented build/test commands land in CLAUDE.md (replacing the placeholder Build & Test section).
notes:
BOOTSTRAP COMPLETE — verified end-to-end under nix develop (clang+gcc+TSan).

BUILD SYSTEM: CMake (3.30.5 from nixpkgs) + Ninja. Library = INTERFACE (header-only) target shimmy::shimmy. tests/ and bench/ are the only compiled targets. Options: SHIMMY_BUILD_TESTS, SHIMMY_BUILD_BENCH, SHIMMY_ENABLE_TSAN.

TEST FRAMEWORK: GoogleTest (gtest 1.15.2 from nixpkgs). Chosen over Catch2 because it pairs with Google Benchmark (same ecosystem), has gtest_discover_tests CMake integration, and good death-test support for the concurrency/TSan work in shimmy-4d4.

GOTCHA — HdrHistogram is NOT in nixpkgs. Built as a pinned source derivation in flake.nix: HdrHistogram/HdrHistogram_c tag 0.11.8, hash sha256-TFlrC4bgK8o5KRZcLMlYU5EO9Oqaqe08PjJgmsUl51M=. title: Core SPMC ring buffer (header-only, in-process)
description: The heart of shimmy: a templated single-producer multi-consumer fixed-block ring buffer, header-only, validated in-process (no shmem dependency for the unit under test). Producer write + publish, consumer zero-copy read with lap detection, sequencing. Both overflow policies (Overwrite, Backpressure) and all wait strategies (Spin, SpinThenYield, Futex) as compile-time tag types.
design:
SLOT-PUBLISH PROTOCOL (the key decision): per-slot atomic<u64> seq stamp acts as a SEQLOCK version. A single trailing release stamp is INSUFFICIENT for the zero-copy Overwrite path — TSan-confirmed torn reads. Producer does TWO-PHASE publish: (1) store(writing_seq, release) BEFORE touching payload, (2) atomic_thread_fence(release), (3) plain stores of len+payload, (4) store(seq, release). writing_seq = ~0-1 sentinel; empty_seq = ~0. Consumer do_read: acquire-load stamp; ==want -> ok; ==writing_seq -> empty(retry); ==empty_seq or <want -> empty; >want -> lapped. validate(): atomic_thread_fence(acquire) THEN relaxed-load stamp, valid iff still ==read_seq_. The acquire FENCE (not an acquire load) is required so payload reads are ordered-before the re-check (acquire load only blocks later ops moving up, wrong direction for seqlock read side).

MEMORY ORDERS (all justified in-code): publish stamp store=release (min to make payload visible; relaxed broken, seq_cst unneeded — no cross-var total order). do_read stamp load=acquire (min; relaxed reads stale payload). validate=acquire FENCE + relaxed load (canonical seqlock read side). next_publish_=relaxed (producer-only; consumers sync on per-slot stamp not this). Backpressure consumer cursors: set_cursor=release / producer await=acquire (lossless handoff edge). register_consumer CAS=acq_rel. Futex: relaxed load to decide whether to sleep (kernel re-reads under futex lock; consumer re-validates with acquire after wake — futex wake is NOT a sync edge for payload).

FALSE-SHARING LAYOUT: next_publish_ on its own cache line; each consumer Cursor alignas(64); each Slot alignas(64). cache_line_size HARD-CODED to 64 (NOT std::hardware_destructive_interference_size) because it participates in the in-shmem byte layout and must NOT vary across gcc/clang/stdlib versions (gcc -Winterference-size ABI warning; libc++ historically missing it). x86-64 phase 1 => 64 is correct.

LAP DETECTION: stamp>want => producer lapped (slot holds want+k*Cap). resync(produced) jumps cursor to produced-Cap (oldest resident). Zero-copy validate catches mid-read lap (stamp changed to writing_seq or newer seq).

ZERO-COPY PAYLOAD RACE: under Overwrite the in-place payload access is the classic seqlock benign race (non-atomic producer write vs consumer read overlap). Correctness comes from validate() discarding torn views, NOT from atomic payload. Under Backpressure no race (slot never overwritten while held). Verified TSan-clean across millions of forced laps on a 2-slot ring; documented as benign race, do not "fix" with atomic payload without measuring.
acceptance_criteria: (1) Header-only, compiles with both gcc & clang C++20. (2) Single-producer/multi-consumer correctness test passes. (3) Overwrite: lapped consumer detects sequence gap, reports loss, resyncs — covered by a test. (4) Backpressure: producer waits for slowest consumer, lossless — covered by a test. (5) Zero-copy read + validate() lap-detection test passes. (6) Concurrency stress under TSan is clean. (7) All 3 wait strategies instantiate and pass a smoke test.
notes:
FILES: include/shimmy/ring.hpp (Ring<BlockSize,Capacity,Overflow,MaxConsumers,Wait> + Consumer<Ring>), include/shimmy/policies.hpp (Overwrite/Backpressure tags), include/shimmy/wait_strategy.hpp (Spin/SpinThenYield<N>/Futex tags), include/shimmy/detail/cache.hpp. Tests: tests/ring_basic_test.cpp, ring_lap_test.cpp, ring_concurrency_test.cpp (wired into shimmy_tests). Bench: bench/ring_bench.cpp (new exe shimmy_ring_bench; minimal hot-path only, full harness=shimmy-7d8).

FOR shimmy-52e (SHARED MEMORY) — DESIGN CONSTRAINTS ALREADY HONORED, MUST PRESERVE:
* Ring has NO internal pointers. Storage = inline std::array<Slot,Capacity>; consumers address slots by index (seq & mask). Consumer holds an integer cursor_id, not a back-pointer to ring state.
* Ring is std::is_standard_layout (asserted in test Layout.RingIsShmemPlaceable), no vtables/virtuals. Can be placement-new-d over an mmap region.
* Ring::init() reinitializes all stamps to empty_seq and zeroes cursors — call it after placement-new over fresh shmem. Has a trailing release fence so a freshly-initialized ring is safely visible.
* All cross-thread state = std::atomic over fixed-width ints => position-independent, identical bytes across processes mapping at different addresses.
* cache_line_size is hard-coded 64 so a gcc producer and clang consumer agree on byte layout. shimmy-52e MUST keep this constant (do not switch to hardware_destructive_interference_size).
* Consumer currently holds Ring* (in-process). shimmy-52e replaces that with an offset-based view but the PROTOCOL is identical — only the addressing changes.
* Backpressure consumer registry (inline cursors[MaxConsumers] with in_use flags) is the seed of the §9 discovery/handshake epic; crash-safety (dead consumer holding a cursor stalls producer forever) is NOT handled — that is deferred per DESIGN §9.

FOR shimmy-7d8 (BENCH HARNESS): hot path is allocation/syscall-free. Sanity floor measured in-process: BM_Publish64 ~1ns (1.02G items/s), BM_PublishReadValidate64 ~1.26ns. These are single-thread microbench floors WITHOUT the pinned-CPU/HdrHistogram rigor — treat as smoke, not headline numbers. Futex wait strategy: producer must call Wait::notify after publish (already wired via if constexpr Wait::blocks). Futex word = low 32 bits of per-slot stamp (asserts little-endian).

VERIFIED: clang 18 + gcc 13 builds clean (-Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic -Wconversion -Wshadow), 18/18 ctest pass both. TSan (clang, SHIMMY_ENABLE_TSAN) 18/18, 0 warnings across 6 repeated runs + dedicated 2-slot maximal-lap zero-copy stress. All inside nix develop.
close_reason:
Implemented the core SPMC fixed-block ring header-only in include/shimmy/ (ring.hpp + policies.hpp + wait_strategy.hpp + detail/cache.hpp), validated in-process. All 7 acceptance criteria met and VERIFIED inside nix develop: (1) header-only, builds clean on clang-18 AND gcc-13 C++20 with -Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic -Wconversion -Wshadow; (2) SPMC correctness — multiple consumers each read the full ordered stream when keeping up; (3) Overwrite lapped consumer detects the sequence gap, reports loss, resyncs to produced-Cap and continues in order; (4) Backpressure is lossless — producer waits on inline producer-visible consumer cursors, every consumer reads all N msgs (total==N*consumers, 0 failures); (5) zero-copy read()+validate() lap detection works, including a deterministic mid-read-lap test and a threaded 2-slot maximal-lap stress; (6) TSan clean — 18/18 tests, 0 warnings across 6 repeated runs; (7) all 3 wait strategies (Spin, SpinThenYield<N>, Futex) instantiate and pass a smoke read.

KEY TECHNICAL DECISION: a single trailing release stamp was INSUFFICIENT (TSan exposed torn/non-monotonic zero-copy reads). Fixed with a two-phase seqlock publish: store(writing_seq, release) -> release fence -> plain payload stores -> store(seq, release); consumer validates with an acquire FENCE + relaxed reload (canonical seqlock read side). Every memory_order is justified in-code. Layout is pointer-free, standard-layout, no vtable, cache-line-isolated (hard-coded 64B), and init()-able over fresh memory — designed so shimmy-52e can place it in shared memory unchanged. Full protocol, ordering rationale, false-sharing layout, and shmem/bench hand-off recorded in --design/--notes. Not committed/pushed per instructions.

comments.author: Dustin
comments.text: Post-close review (main session, independently re-verified clang/gcc/TSan all 18/18, 0 TSan warnings across repeated runs — agent report holds). Found one undocumented operational contract, now documented in ring.hpp register_consumer(): BACKPRESSURE requires all consumers to register BEFORE the producer publishes its Capacity-th message. A late-joining Backpressure consumer registers with cursor=0 but seq 0's slot may already be overwritten -> producer deadlocks in await_consumers_past() waiting on a cursor that can never advance, while the consumer gets 'lapped' (which Backpressure doesn't handle). The tests sidestep this with a start barrier; the contract was real but unwritten. Code is correct as tested; this is a precondition, not a bug. Overwrite is unaffected (late consumers resync). No behavior change made — comment-only.

title: Benchmark + analytics harness (data backbone)
description: The 'data doesn't lie' backbone, built alongside the core (not after). Compiled bench targets producing HdrHistogram latency distributions (p50..p99.99), throughput sweeps across message size and consumer count, perf-counter capture, CSV output, and a CI regression gate. This is what converts every design claim into measured data.
design:
BENCH HARNESS ARCHITECTURE (shimmy-7d8). Three new compiled targets in bench/ + a python gate, all standalone (NOT Google Benchmark driver programs — they need precise control of the timed window and pinned producer/consumer threads which the GBench loop obscures).

FILES ADDED: bench/common.hpp (timing+pinning+CPU-noise-detect+HdrHistogram RAII+CSV writer+arg parse), bench/latency_bench.cpp (shimmy_latency_bench), bench/throughput_bench.cpp (shimmy_throughput_bench), bench/check_regression.py (gate), bench/baselines/{throughput.csv,latency.csv,README.md}. MODIFIED: bench/CMakeLists.txt (+2 exes, find_package(Threads)), .github/workflows/ci.yml (+bench job), .gitignore (ignore root-level run CSVs, keep bench/baselines tracked).

LATENCY METHODOLOGY: end-to-end producer-publish -> consumer-visible. Producer writes steady_clock send-ts into first 8B of payload (the memcpy we already do), publishes; consumer zero-copy reads, takes recv-ts the instant it observes, reads send-ts back, validate()s, records recv-send-clock_overhead into HdrHistogram. Same clock/host (single NUMA, DESIGN §8) so no clock-domain crossing. Producer/consumer pinned to DISTINCT physical cores (cpu0/cpu2) so latency reflects real inter-core coherence not SMT-sibling shared-L1. Producer PACED (busy-wait, default 2us gap) so consumer keeps up and we measure publish->visible not queueing; pacing also parks the consumer on each msg, exercising the wait strategy wakeup cost (the whole point of per-tier comparison). Backpressure policy so nothing is lost. Warmup discards first N samples; clock overhead measured (min back-to-back read) and subtracted. Run ONCE PER WAIT STRATEGY (Spin/SpinThenYield/Futex), each tail reported SEPARATELY (DESIGN §6, not collapsed).

THROUGHPUT METHODOLOGY: single producer, Overwrite policy (throughput-optimal, producer never blocks => measured rate is producer ceiling not consumer-gated = the real single-producer number). Sweep block {64,256,1024,4096} (compile-time NTTP, templated run_cell) x consumers {0,1,2,4} (real pinned reader threads draining => includes fan-out coherence cost; 0=pure publish ceiling). Time ONLY the publish loop (steady_clock around N publishes) after warmup + full-capacity pre-touch (no page faults in timed window). reps=N, keep BEST (min-time, standard for ceiling microbench; mean folds in scheduler noise we exclude). Ring heap-allocated (4096x4KB ring ~17MB overflows stack).

CSV SCHEMA (stable, leading kind column so one parser reads both): latency.csv = kind,wait_strategy,block_size,samples,clock_overhead_ns,p50_ns,p90_ns,p99_ns,p999_ns,p9999_ns,min_ns,max_ns,mean_ns,governor,turbo,noisy. throughput.csv = kind,policy,wait_strategy,block_size,consumers,messages,elapsed_ns,throughput_msgs_per_sec,throughput_mib_per_sec,governor,turbo,noisy. Gate keys throughput on (block_size,consumers), latency on (wait_strategy,block_size) — match by dimension not line order.

REGRESSION GATE (bench/check_regression.py): throughput regression = drop >20%; latency regression = p99.9 increase >50% (tails far noisier, looser bound). ADVISORY by default (exit0, print deltas), HARD only with --strict (exit1). DECISION hard-vs-advisory: CI runs on GitHub-hosted runners with NO perf governor / NO core isolation / noisy neighbours = exactly the config the harness flags noisy=1 and which tails cannot be trusted on; a hard gate there flaps and erodes 'data doesnt lie'. So CI = advisory (surfaces drift, never blocks on runner noise); maintainer regenerates baseline on pinned quiet machine and runs --strict to truly gate. EMPIRICALLY VALIDATED: a fresh CI-scale run on this noisy box showed a uniform ~30% throughput drop across nearly all cells (global downclock/contention, NOT a code change) — advisory correctly did not block; that is precisely the false-positive a hard gate would have fired. NOISY-GUARD: if current run noisy=1, --strict downgrades regression to WARN (exit0) unless --ignore-noise. Verified all paths: pass(RC0), strict-catch(RC1 throughput & latency), advisory(RC0 with regression), noisy-downgrade(RC0 WARN).
acceptance_criteria: (1) Latency bench emits HdrHistogram p50..p99.99 per wait strategy. (2) Throughput sweep emits CSV for 64B/256B/1KB/4KB. (3) Headline number confirms >=10M msgs/sec @64B single producer (or reports the measured figure + sets the gate from it). (4) Per-tier p99.9 published: Spin/SpinThenYield/Futex each benched against its own impl. (5) CI regression gate fails the build on a meaningful slowdown vs baseline. (6) Output is reproducible run-to-run on the pinned toolchain.
notes:
MEASURED NUMBERS (clang-18 Release, pinned Nix devshell, AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370, 12c/24t single NUMA, x86-64; governor=POWERSAVE turbo=ON => harness flags noisy=1, tails indicative not authoritative; could NOT set performance governor — no passwordless sudo — measured honestly and flagged).

HEADLINE THROUGHPUT @64B single producer: 151 M msgs/sec with 1 live consumer => DESIGN §5 target >=10M MET by ~15x. Pure publish ceiling (0 consumers) @64B: 0.78-1.09 BILLION msgs/sec (65 GiB/s). Sanity floor matches shimmy-4d4 smoke (BM_Publish64 ~0.92ns = 1.09G/s).

FULL THROUGHPUT CURVE (msgs/sec, committed baseline, 1 consumer): 64B=151M, 256B=90.8M, 1KB=65.5M, 4KB=12.2M (=47.6 GiB/s, memcpy-bandwidth bound). Degrades cleanly with size (memcpy) and consumer count (coherence): @64B 0c=777M 1c=151M 2c=93.5M 4c=14.7M. Big blocks bandwidth-bound: 4KB at any consumer count ~12-48 GiB/s. The msgs/sec headline is for small blocks; for large blocks the MiB/s column is the real story.

PER-WAIT-STRATEGY p99.9 vs DESIGN §6 HYPOTHESES (best/cleanest run, 300k samples):
 * Spin:          p50=30 p90=40 p99=41 p99.9=311ns  | hyp <200ns: p99 MEETS, p99.9 slightly over (noise). Median path firmly in spec.
 * SpinThenYield: p50=40 p90=41 p99=50 p99.9=631ns  | hyp <1us: MET at p99.9.
 * Futex:         p50=701 p99=2013 p99.9=36767ns    | hyp <10us: p50/p99 MEET, p99.9 MISSES on this noisy box (syscall wakeup tail under powersave).
HONEST CAVEAT: p99.9+ varies run-to-run by 10-100x on this powersave/contended box (Spin p99.9 seen anywhere 311ns..38us across runs); p50/p90/p99 are STABLE and trustworthy, p99.9 is noise-dominated here. The harness correctly flags noisy=1 every run. On a pinned quiet machine the spin tails should tighten toward the hypotheses — re-run with governor=performance + isolcpus to get authoritative tails (deferred: needs privileged host).

SECONDARY FINDING (worth a future bead): SpinThenYield<1024> under a PACED producer on a busy box can spike p99.9 to ~25-390us in some runs — when the consumer exhausts its 1024-iter spin budget between paced messages and sched_yield()s, the scheduler can park it for a long stretch on a 24-thread powersave box. Spin and Futex are more robust to this. The SpinLimit and the yield-vs-futex crossover are tuning knobs worth characterizing on a quiet machine (DESIGN §10 open question).

HOW TO RUN: nix develop --command bash -c 'cmake -S . -B build -G Ninja -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release && cmake --build build'. Then:
 ./build/bench/shimmy_latency_bench --samples=300000 --warmup=30000 --gap_ns=2000 --csv=latency.csv
 ./build/bench/shimmy_throughput_bench --messages=10000000 --warmup=200000 --reps=4 --csv=throughput.csv
 python3 bench/check_regression.py --baseline bench/baselines/throughput.csv --current throughput.csv [--strict]
CLI flags: latency --samples/--warmup/--gap_ns/--csv ; throughput --messages/--warmup/--reps/--csv. Both print a CPU-config banner + per-target verdicts to stderr and the CSV table to stdout+file.

BUILD/COMPILER: benchmarked under clang-18 -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release (Release matters — RelWithDebInfo is the CMake default but bench numbers are Release). Both new harnesses ALSO build+run clean under gcc-13 Release (verified, 0 warnings under -Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic -Wconversion -Wshadow; gcc 64B/1c short run ~100M/s, consistent). 18/18 ctest pass unchanged. Smoke benches (shimmy_bench, shimmy_ring_bench) still run.

NOT committed/pushed per worktree instructions — main session merges.
close_reason:
Built the data backbone: 3 compiled targets + a regression gate, all verified by RUNNING under nix develop (clang-18 Release; also gcc-13 clean). ADDED bench/common.hpp, bench/latency_bench.cpp (shimmy_latency_bench), bench/throughput_bench.cpp (shimmy_throughput_bench), bench/check_regression.py, bench/baselines/{throughput,latency}.csv + README; MODIFIED bench/CMakeLists.txt, .github/workflows/ci.yml (advisory bench job + CSV artifact), .gitignore.

KEY MEASURED NUMBERS (AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370, single NUMA, x86-64; governor=POWERSAVE turbo=ON => harness flags noisy=1, no sudo to fix — measured honestly):
 * HEADLINE throughput @64B single producer, 1 consumer = 151 M msgs/sec => DESIGN §5 target >=10M MET ~15x. Pure publish ceiling @64B = 0.78-1.09 BILLION/s. Full sweep curve (1c): 64B=151M 256B=90.8M 1KB=65.5M 4KB=12.2M(47.6GiB/s); plus 0/2/4-consumer fan-out cost — all in throughput.csv.
 * Per-wait-strategy p99.9 vs DESIGN §6: Spin p50=30/p99=41/p99.9=311ns (hyp<200ns: p99 MEETS, p99.9 just over on noise); SpinThenYield p99.9=631ns (hyp<1us: MET); Futex p50=701/p99=2013/p99.9=36.7us (hyp<10us: p50/p99 MEET, p99.9 MISSES on noisy box). Each tier benched against its OWN impl, reported separately (not collapsed).

ACCEPTANCE: (1) HdrHistogram p50..p99.99 per wait strategy — YES. (2) throughput CSV sweep 64B/256B/1KB/4KB — YES. (3) >=10M @64B confirmed (151M) — YES. (4) per-tier p99.9 published Spin/SpinThenYield/Futex — YES. (5) CI gate fails on regression — YES, verified catching synthetic throughput AND latency regressions under --strict (RC=1) while advisory by default (RC=0); chose advisory-on-CI/strict-on-pinned-machine and EMPIRICALLY VALIDATED the choice (a real ~30% noise blip on this box did NOT block, as intended). (6) reproducible on pinned toolchain — YES (median path + min-time throughput stable; p99.9 tails noise-dominated on this powersave host, flagged noisy=1 every run).

HONEST CAVEATS recorded in --notes: p99.9+ varies 10-100x run-to-run on this noisy/powersave/contended host — p50/p90/p99 trustworthy, p99.9 indicative. Authoritative tails need a pinned quiet machine => filed follow-up shimmy-cvs (also covers a SpinThenYield<1024> tail-spike finding). Not committed/pushed — main session merges the worktree.

title: shimmy core: SPMC shared-memory messaging (phase 1)
description: Build the phase-1 core of shimmy: a single-host, SPMC, shared-memory messaging library in C++20. A 'channel' is a fixed-block-size ring buffer (block size chosen at compile time), single producer fanning out to many consumers, variable-but-bounded payloads, zero-copy reads with lap detection. Header-only library + compiled benchmark/analytics harness. Linux-only, x86-64, single NUMA node. See DESIGN.md for full rationale and decisions. Guiding principle: all performance claims must be backed by benchmark data — speculation is useless.
design: Fixed-size-slot ring (not bip buffer): block = [u32 len][payload][unused tail], variable payload within fixed block. Power-of-2 capacity -> index by mask. Compile-time axes: BlockSize (NTTP), Capacity, overflow policy (Overwrite|Backpressure tag), max-consumers, wait strategy (Spin|SpinThenYield|Futex). Zero-copy read returns span into shmem + validate() rechecks sequence for lap detection. Both overflow policies supported per-channel (doubles test surface, accepted). x86-64 TSO assumed on common path but atomics expressed via correct C++ memory_order. Full design narrative in DESIGN.md (committed to repo as the durable design doc alongside this epic).
acceptance_criteria: Phase 1 complete when: (1) core SPMC ring implemented header-only with both overflow policies and all 3 wait strategies; (2) shared-memory segment lifecycle implemented (shm_open/mmap, versioned header, hugepage option); (3) benchmark+analytics harness produces HdrHistogram latency + throughput sweep CSV in CI with a regression gate; (4) measured throughput >=10M msgs/sec single-producer @64B; (5) p99.9 latency benched and published per wait-strategy tier (Spin<200ns, SpinThenYield<1us, Futex<10us as hypotheses to confirm); (6) full bench curve published for 64B/256B/1KB/4KB.
close_reason: Phase 1 complete: all 4 children closed and verified in the merged tree. Core SPMC ring (4d4), shared-memory segment lifecycle (52e), and benchmark/analytics harness (7d8) all integrated; bootstrap (1wv) green. Merged-tree verification by main session: clang 26/26, gcc 26/26, TSan 25/25 (fork test gated, documented) with 0 ThreadSanitizer warnings. Measured headline throughput 103-151M msgs/sec @64B single-producer/1-consumer -> >=10M target MET ~10-15x. Per-wait-strategy latency tiers benched (Spin/SpinThenYield/Futex) per DESIGN §6; authoritative p99.9 requires a pinned quiet host -> follow-up shimmy-cvs filed. Backpressure cross-process limited to attach-at-current-seq; lossless-from-0 dynamic attach + crash-safety deferred to shimmy-uud. Nothing committed/pushed per user.

title: Investigate coherence-traffic reduction to raise small-message throughput
description: Small-message (<=256B) single-producer throughput is CACHE-COHERENCE BOUND (DESIGN.md §5a, bd memory): the bottleneck is the cache-line migrating producer-core -> reader-core when a freshly-published slot is read, NOT CPU clock or instruction count (proven: +41% clock gave 0 headline movement). To raise small-message throughput we must reduce coherence traffic / cache-line migrations on the hot path. Investigate candidate techniques and MEASURE each on the existing harness — speculation is useless, data decides. This is the single highest-leverage throughput lever we have.
design: Candidate techniques to evaluate (each must answer: does it reduce hot-path cache-line migrations?): (1) batch the producer's stamp/payload so multiple messages share one line migration; (2) cache-line packing — multiple small messages per cache line vs current one-slot-per-line, trading false-sharing risk vs migration count; (3) relaxing the per-slot stamp granularity (e.g. stamp groups) — but must NOT break the seqlock validate() lap-detection (see shimmy-4d4); (4) prefetch / non-temporal store hints on the producer write; (5) consumer read batching that keeps a line resident longer. Measure each variant's 64B/1c and 64B/2c throughput + the latency tails against baseline. Tie to the batch-sync findings (shimmy-68t): batch-consume gave NO Overwrite throughput win precisely because it didn't touch the migrating slot line — so the winning technique must target that line specifically.
acceptance_criteria: (1) A written findings report ranking candidate techniques by MEASURED 64B throughput delta (1c and 2c) on the existing harness, with noisy-config caveat. (2) At least one prototyped technique with before/after numbers. (3) A clear recommendation: which technique(s) to productionize, expected gain, and any correctness/latency tradeoff (esp. seqlock lap-detection must remain intact). (4) If no technique meaningfully helps, that is a valid documented outcome. All numbers from actual runs.
notes: Depends conceptually on the DESIGN §5a coherence-bound analysis. Authoritative tail measurements need shimmy-cvs (quiet host). Any winning technique must preserve the two-phase seqlock publish + per-message validate() contract from shimmy-4d4.

title: Skip two-phase writing_seq marker under Backpressure (compile-time elide)
description: Ring::publish unconditionally emits PHASE-1 slot.seq.store(writing_seq, release) + a release fence before the payload stores. The writing_seq seqlock marker only exists to make zero-copy reads correct under OVERWRITE (where the producer can overwrite an unread slot mid-read). Under Backpressure await_consumers_past guarantees the slot is already free before publish touches it, so the slot is stable and the marker buys nothing — it is an extra atomic store to slot.seq plus an extra observable stamp transition (writing_seq->seq) that every consumer's do_read must filter. The ring.hpp file header itself states: 'Under Backpressure the producer never overwrites an unread slot, so the zero-copy view is fully stable and there is no race at all.' Gate PHASE-1 (and its fence) behind if constexpr(is_same_v<Overflow, Overwrite>).
design: Wrap lines ~223-229 of ring.hpp (the writing_seq store + release fence) in if constexpr(std::is_same_v<Overflow, Overwrite>). Backpressure publish then collapses to: payload stores + single release stamp. do_read's stamp==writing_seq branch can stay (harmless for Backpressure since it never fires) or also be reasoned about, but leaving it is safe. This does NOT change Overwrite behavior at all (the headline coherence-bound path is untouched). It targets the Backpressure path, which is exactly what latency_bench uses (Ring<64,1024,Backpressure,...>), so the extra store+transition removal should show in the publish->visible latency tail.
acceptance_criteria: (1) writing_seq marker + fence compile-time eliminated for Backpressure; still emitted for Overwrite. (2) Overwrite zero-copy lap-detection / validate() / tiny-ring forced-lap stress tests UNCHANGED and still TSan-clean. (3) Backpressure correctness tests still pass; TSan-clean (run ring_concurrency under both policies). (4) All 26 tests pass clang+gcc. (5) MEASURED on latency_bench under Backpressure: report p50/p99/p99.9 delta vs before (expect small improvement or flat — must not regress). (6) Verdict with data.
close_reason: Elided the two-phase writing_seq marker + release fence under Backpressure via if constexpr(Overwrite). The marker only guards the Overwrite lap-mid-read seqlock race, which await_consumers_past makes impossible under Backpressure. Overwrite path unchanged (byte-identical, TSan-clean seqlock stressor still passes); Backpressure publish drops one atomic store + one fence. All 26 tests pass clang+gcc, TSan-clean. Measured no regression on a noisy host; authoritative latency delta deferred to shimmy-cvs (quiet-machine rerun).

comments.author: Dustin
comments.text:
Implemented: gated PHASE-1 writing_seq marker + release fence behind if constexpr(is_same_v<Overflow,Overwrite>) in ring.hpp publish(). Backpressure publish now: payload stores + single release stamp (one fewer atomic store + one fewer fence per publish).

VALIDATION:
- All 26 tests pass under clang AND gcc.
- TSan-clean (25/25): ZeroCopyInPlaceUnderMaximalLapping (Overwrite seqlock stressor, tiny Cap=2, 300k forced laps) unchanged + still clean -> proves Overwrite path byte-identical; BackpressureLosslessFanout (3 consumers, 50k msgs) clean under the elided path.
- Overwrite path is literally unchanged (the marker still emitted there) -> headline coherence-bound 64B throughput unaffected, as predicted.

MEASUREMENT (latency_bench, Backpressure, 200k samples): host is NOISY (governor=powersave, turbo ON), so per harness+DESIGN the tails are indicative only. p50 ~40-50ns consistent with committed baseline (~30ns); Spin p99.9 dominated by scheduler context-switch noise (~1ms), not signal. No regression observed; a clear win cannot be demonstrated on this host. Authoritative delta deferred to the quiet-machine rerun (shimmy-cvs). The change is a strict reduction in producer work and is correctness-proven, so it stands on that basis.

title: Conditional futex wake: skip FUTEX_WAKE syscall when no consumer is parked
description: Ring::publish currently issues FUTEX_WAKE_PRIVATE (INT_MAX) after EVERY publish under the Futex wait strategy (wait_strategy.hpp Futex::notify, called from ring.hpp publish). When the producer outpaces a parked consumer this is the common keeping-up case, the wake is a wasted syscall (hundreds of ns) on the producer hot path because no consumer is actually blocked. Add a waiter-count (or single waiting-flag) atomic that a Futex consumer increments before FUTEX_WAIT and decrements after, so the producer can skip the syscall with a relaxed load when the count is zero. Distinct from shimmy-qui (batch-publish API): this is per-publish conditional notify, not N->1 wakeup collapse; they compose.
design: Add an atomic waiters counter to the Ring, cache-line isolated from producer/consumer state. Futex::wait increments before sleeping, decrements on return (RAII guard or explicit). Producer: if constexpr(Wait::blocks) { if (waiters.load(relaxed)) Wait::notify(...); }. The waiters counter lives in the ring (shared across processes) so it must be in the on-segment layout -> changes sizeof(Ring) -> bumps SegmentHeader ring_sizeof fingerprint (an opener built without it correctly rejects an old segment, which is the desired fail-loud). Memory ordering: the inc must be visible to the producer's relaxed check before the consumer commits to FUTEX_WAIT, and the producer's publish (release stamp) must be ordered before its waiters load — standard futex eventcount discipline; verify there is no lost-wakeup window (consumer: inc, re-check seq, then WAIT with cmp; producer: publish, then check waiters).
acceptance_criteria: (1) Futex producer skips notify() syscall when waiters==0, issues it when >0. (2) No lost-wakeup: a consumer that decides to block is always woken — stress test with producer racing a consumer that parks/unparks, TSan-clean. (3) Existing 26 tests still pass under clang+gcc and TSan. (4) MEASURED: strace/perf shows FUTEX_WAKE syscall count drops to ~0 in the keeping-up case on latency_bench under Futex; report producer-side delta. (5) SegmentHeader fingerprint bump verified to reject old/new layout mismatch.
close_reason: Implemented eventcount conditional futex wake: producer skips FUTEX_WAKE when waiters_==0. Measured 99.9% reduction in producer wake syscalls (22000->20 over ~22k msgs in the keep-up case) with Futex p99 dropping ~8.8us->50ns on a noisy host; exact syscall delta via LD_PRELOAD counter. Lost-wakeup freedom via seq_cst eventcount discipline, proven race-free under TSan and by 2 new paced-park stress tests (Backpressure + Overwrite) that would hang on a lost wake. All tests pass clang/gcc/TSan. ABI fingerprint (ring_sizeof) correctly bumped to fail-loud on old<->new segment mismatch. Distinct from and composable with shimmy-qui (batch-publish N->1 wake collapse).

comments.author: Dustin
comments.text:
Implemented eventcount conditional wake. Ring gains atomic<int32_t> waiters_ (own cache line). Futex::wait does seq_cst waiters++ -> seq_cst stamp re-load -> FUTEX_WAIT -> waiters-- ; Futex::notify does seq_cst load(waiters), returns without syscall if 0. Spin/SpinThenYield take the waiters param and ignore it (uniform tag-dispatch signature). Lost-wakeup freedom argued via the single seq_cst total order (see wait_strategy.hpp struct comment): if producer skips the wake it must precede the consumer's increment in that order, so the consumer's re-load sees the publish and does not sleep.

VALIDATION:
- 2 new TSan stress tests (ring_concurrency): FutexConditionalWakeNoLostWakeup (Backpressure, paced producer forces real FUTEX_WAIT parking, asserts all N arrive -> a lost wakeup would hang before N) and FutexConditionalWakeOverwriteProgress. Both reach N.
- All tests pass: clang 28/28, gcc 28/28, TSan 27/27 (fork test compiled out under TSan). TSan-clean proves the seq_cst eventcount has no data race.

MEASURED (LD_PRELOAD futex-syscall counter, latency_bench Futex cell, ~22000 published msgs, keep-up gap=200ns):
  GATED (new):         FUTEX_WAKE=20    FUTEX_WAIT=4   p50=30ns  p99=50ns
  UNCONDITIONAL (old): FUTEX_WAKE=22000 FUTEX_WAIT=4   p50=30ns  p99=8839ns
-> 99.9% of producer FUTEX_WAKE syscalls eliminated (one-per-publish -> ~zero) in the keep-up case. Even on this noisy host Futex p99 fell ~8.8us -> 50ns. Host is noisy (powersave/turbo) so absolute tails indicative, but the syscall-count delta is exact and decisive.

FINGERPRINT (criterion 5): waiters_ changes sizeof(Ring); SegmentHeader.ring_sizeof check in validate_header rejects old<->new layout mismatch, covered by existing OpenRejectsMismatchedInstantiation. A/B escape-hatch macro used for the measurement was removed; no temp build dirs left.

comments.author: Dustin
comments.text: PERF REGRESSION FOUND AND FIXED (post-merge A/B validation). Placing waiters_ BETWEEN next_publish_ and cursors_/slots_ shifted the slot array offset and cost ~15% on the single-producer publish ceiling (64B/0-consumer): before median ~1090-1108M vs after ~944-951M, cleanly-separated ranges (no overlap), interleaved + repeated to cancel drift. Root cause isolated by A/B/C: a third variant with waiters_ moved to the struct TAIL fully recovered throughput (~1101M), proving it was layout, not the marker if-constexpr or notify guard (both codegen-neutral for Overwrite). FIX: moved waiters_ to the end of the Ring (after slots_) so next_publish_/cursors_/slots_ keep original offsets; the unused-by-spinners word sits at the tail. Re-validated: clang/gcc 28/28, TSan 27/27 still pass; throughput recovered to within run-to-run noise of before (overlapping ranges). Lesson: ANY new Ring member must go after slots_, never ahead of the hot-path fields. Measured on a NOISY host (powersave/turbo, + active vesktop) so absolute numbers indicative, but the before-fix 15% drop was non-overlapping and the post-fix recovery is unambiguous.

title: Wire LLVM source-based coverage into build (SHIMMY_ENABLE_COVERAGE)
description: Add a clang source-based coverage path so the README and CI can report a real measured library-coverage %. Header-only lib means coverage is realized through the compiled test TUs that include the headers. Implemented this session: SHIMMY_ENABLE_COVERAGE CMake option in tests/CMakeLists.txt (mirrors SHIMMY_ENABLE_TSAN), llvmPackages_18.libllvm (llvm-cov + llvm-profdata, version-matched to clang 18) added to flake.nix commonTools.
acceptance_criteria: (1) cmake -DSHIMMY_ENABLE_COVERAGE=ON builds the test target with -fprofile-instr-generate -fcoverage-mapping inside nix develop. (2) ctest produces profraw; llvm-profdata merge + llvm-cov report yields a coverage figure over include/shimmy/*. (3) README records the measured % with reproduce steps. DONE: measured 84.99% lines / 87.03% regions / 74.49% branches over exercised library headers (ring/shm_segment/wait_strategy), 26 tests pass.

title: Productionize batch-consume as opt-in Backpressure latency tool
description: Follow-up from shimmy-68t. The batch-consume prototype (Consumer::read_batch/Batch on branch investigate/batch-amortized-sync) amortizes the consumer-cursor STORE + produced() acquire over a run while keeping per-slot validate() per-message. Investigation found the win is specifically on the Backpressure/cursor-store path (the contended line is the consumer cursor the producer reads every slot-reuse); on the Overwrite-throughput path batch drain LOWERS producer rate by 28-34% @64B because Overwrite consumers publish no cursor (nothing to amortize). So productionize batch-consume as an OPT-IN latency/tail tool for Backpressure, and keep it OFF the Overwrite-throughput default path.
design: Keep additive (per-message API intact). The branch already has the prototype + 6 batch correctness tests (TSan-clean incl. maximal-lapping) + harness mode flags. Productionization = docs + API stabilization + the Overwrite-throughput 'do not use here' guidance, not new mechanism. Depends on shimmy-cvs for the authoritative light-load tail verdict.
acceptance_criteria: (1) read_batch/Batch promoted from prototype to a documented, supported API with a clear note that it targets the Backpressure latency path and is NOT recommended for Overwrite throughput drain (data: -28..34% @64B). (2) The stable +40-60ns median cost and the heavy-load tail-stability documented in the API docs. (3) Existing per-message API unchanged; all tests + TSan still pass. (4) Ideally gated/recommended behind quiet-host tail confirmation from shimmy-cvs (light-load tail-down currently unconfirmed on noisy box).

title: Batch-publish (Futex-tier): collapse N wakeups into 1, keep per-slot stamps
description: Follow-up from shimmy-68t batch-amortized-sync investigation. Prototype batch-PUBLISH on the producer side: write N payloads, then publish with fewer SHARED ops. The investigation resolved the visibility-model fork by reasoning+consume-side data: KEEP per-slot seqlock stamps; do NOT add/replace with a shared monotonic published-counter (a shared counter breaks zero-copy lap-detection, which keys on per-slot stamps, and cannot eliminate the per-slot stamp stores that correctness requires). On x86-64 TSO the per-slot release stamps are already plain MOVs (cheap), so the only genuinely-shared batch-publish saving is collapsing N Futex wakeup syscalls into 1 (a syscall-count win, not a coherence one) plus one next_publish_ advance. So batch-publish is only worth it under the Futex wait strategy.
design: Visibility fork already decided in shimmy-68t: per-slot stamps stay (option A), no shared counter (option B rejected). See docs/batch-sync-findings.md §5. The notify() in Futex is the one shared/expensive op per publish; batch it. Spin/SpinThenYield tiers have no wakeup syscall so batch-publish offers them little — measure Futex specifically.
acceptance_criteria: (1) A batch-publish API (e.g. publish_batch / reserve N + commit) that keeps per-slot stamps as lap-detection source of truth (NO shared counter). (2) Correctness: existing Overwrite lap-detection + zero-copy validate() tests still pass + TSan-clean under batch-publish. (3) MEASURED on the latency harness under FUTEX: does collapsing N wakeups into 1 reduce the Futex p99.9 wakeup tail vs per-message publish? Report the delta. (4) Clear verdict: pursue or drop, with data. If the wakeup collapse does not pay on a quiet host, drop it.

title: Re-run latency tails on a pinned, quiet machine for authoritative p99.9
description: The shimmy-7d8 latency tails (p99.9+) were measured on a noisy host (governor=powersave, turbo on, 24-thread contention, no core isolation — no privileged access to fix). p50/p90/p99 are stable and in-spec; p99.9 varied run-to-run by 10-100x and is noise-dominated, so the DESIGN §6 per-tier verdicts (Spin<200ns, SpinThenYield<1us, Futex<10us) could only be confirmed at p99 / on best runs. Re-run on a machine where governor=performance, turbo disabled, and the producer/consumer cores are isolated (isolcpus=) to get authoritative tails and either confirm or refute the §6 hypotheses, then regenerate bench/baselines/ with noisy=0 and switch CI's maintainer-side gate to --strict.
acceptance_criteria: (1) Latency harness re-run on a host with governor=performance + turbo off + isolated producer/consumer cores (harness reports noisy=0). (2) Per-strategy p99.9 recorded and compared to DESIGN §6 hypotheses with a clear met/missed verdict per tier. (3) bench/baselines/{latency,throughput}.csv regenerated on that host (noisy=0) and committed. (4) Decision recorded on whether the §6 tail targets hold; if a tier misses on clean hardware, file the optimization or revise the hypothesis.
notes: Harness + CSV schema + gate already built in shimmy-7d8; this is purely a measurement-environment task. Also investigate the SpinThenYield<1024> tail spike (p99.9 up to ~25-390us under a paced producer on a busy box — consumer exhausts spin budget then sched_yield parks long). Characterize SpinLimit and the yield-vs-futex crossover (DESIGN §10 open question). Use: ./build/bench/shimmy_latency_bench --samples=300000 --gap_ns=2000 --csv=... and check the stderr noisy banner reads clean.

comments.author: Dustin
comments.text: From shimmy-68t (batch-amortized-sync investigation): the batch-consume LIGHT-LOAD tail-down prediction (median up / p99.9 down) could NOT be confirmed on this noisy box — permsg p99.9 swings 0.24us..110us for the SAME config across runs, so batch sometimes wins big, sometimes loses run-to-run. When this quiet-host re-measure happens, ALSO run shimmy_latency_bench (which now has --mode permsg|batch built in via the new mode/gap_ns CSV columns) at gap=1000 and gap=0 to get an authoritative batch-vs-permsg tail delta. The stable, confirmed effect is the +40-60ns median rise; the heavy-load (gap=0) batch path already shows a tight/stable p99.9 (~14-23us) vs the violent permsg tail (~9-305us) even on the noisy box — quiet host should sharpen that.

comments.author: Dustin
comments.text: Baselines regenerated on the AMD performance power profile (EPP=performance) this session. IMPORTANT: this is NOT the authoritative turbo-off run this bead tracks — governor string still reads 'powersave' and boost is still ON, so harness correctly stamps noisy=1. The performance profile DID measurably help: Futex p99.9 36767ns->2415ns, SpinThenYield p99.9 631ns->251ns, Spin p99.9 311ns->240ns; throughput 0-consumer drain 64B 777M->1097M msgs/sec; headline 64B/1c essentially flat 151.0M->151.1M (it's coherence/bandwidth-bound, not freq-bound). Committed bench/baselines/{throughput,latency}.csv from a representative 300k-sample run (4 runs taken; p50/p90/p99 rock-stable, p99.9 stable-ish, p99.99 still swings 10x+ esp Futex 12647-120063ns -> that's the host jitter this bead must eliminate). Authoritative turbo-off + isolcpus re-measure STILL PENDING here. Also: added pinned python+matplotlib to flake.nix and bench/plot.py to regenerate assets/*.svg from the CSVs.

title: Shared-memory segment lifecycle
description: Place the core ring in a named POSIX shared-memory segment so it works across processes. shm_open/mmap, a versioned segment header (magic + layout version), alignment, optional hugepages (MAP_HUGETLB), and teardown/unlink ownership semantics.
design:
SEGMENT LAYOUT: [SegmentHeader | padding | Ring] in one mmap. Header at offset 0 (fixed-width POD, standard-layout, trivially-copyable, pointer-free). Ring placed at ring_offset() = round_up(sizeof(SegmentHeader), alignof(Ring)); alignof(Ring)==64 (cache-line). Creator and opener BOTH compute this offset from compile-time constants, so they agree WITHOUT storing any pointer => address-independence preserved (two processes may mmap at different vaddrs; Ring is already index/offset-based, no absolute pointers added).

HEADER/VERSION/COMPAT: SegmentHeader{magic(0x534849...), version=1, header_bytes, block_size, capacity, policy(policy_id enum:overwrite=1/backpressure=2), max_consumers, ring_sizeof, ring_alignof, slot_sizeof, state}. open() validates EVERY field against the opener's OWN compile-time Ring instantiation via check_eq; ANY mismatch (magic/version/blocksize/capacity/policy/maxconsumers/struct-size/offset) throws shm_layout_error. Struct-size fingerprints (ring_sizeof/alignof/slot_sizeof) catch ABI/padding disagreements the named params miss. NEVER reinterprets an incompatible layout. open() of nonexistent name -> shm_os_error(ENOENT).

CREATE vs OPEN: create() = shm_open(O_CREAT|O_RDWR|O_TRUNC)->ftruncate(segment_bytes)->mmap(MAP_SHARED)->memset header->fill fields(state=Initializing)->placement-new Ring (ctor calls init(): resets stamps/cursors + trailing release fence)->release fence->state=Ready. open() = shm_open(O_RDWR)->fstat(size guard)->mmap->validate_header (rejects if state!=Ready, i.e. creator mid-init/crashed)->std::launder existing Ring in place (NO placement-new on open: that would re-init and stomp live data). Creator/opener readiness handshake: creator writes all header fields + constructs ring, release fence, THEN state=Ready; opener reads state, acquire fence, then reads fields => observes a fully-initialized segment iff it sees Ready.

ALIGNMENT: ring_offset rounds sizeof(header) up to alignof(Ring)=64; header is 80 bytes -> ring at offset 128. Header never misaligns the ring.

UNLINK OWNERSHIP / RAII: ShmSegment<RingT> is move-only RAII owning {ring_ ptr, map_, map_bytes_, fd_, owns_name_, name_}. reset(): munmap + close(fd) always; shm_unlink(name) ONLY if owns_name_ (creator). Opener never unlinks a name it does not own. Exceptions mid-create/open unwind the RAII members (no leak on error path). Verified: /dev/shm has NO shimmy_* after full test run; second shm_open w/o O_CREAT -> ENOENT after creator dtor.

HUGEPAGES: opt-in segment_options::hugepages. POSIX shm objects cannot be hugepage-backed via shm_open/ftruncate; the portable path is anonymous MAP_SHARED|MAP_ANONYMOUS|MAP_HUGETLB (fork-shared, NOT name-discoverable, so owns_name_ cleared). Best-effort: on MAP_FAILED (no reserved hugepages) returns false and create() falls back to the normal shm path; hugepages_active()==false. Never hard-fails a normal run. Length rounded up to 2MiB. Tail-latency measurement is shimmy-7d8's job.

BACKPRESSURE CROSS-PROCESS STANCE (chosen): Overwrite fully supported cross-process (late opener resyncs on lap, address boundaries change nothing). Backpressure's lossless-from-seq-0 guarantee is NOT supported cross-process this cut (cannot enforce 'all consumers joined before Capacity-th publish' across processes -> the documented producer-deadlock hazard). Safe path: ShmSegment::attach_consumer_at_current() -> Consumer.seek(ring.produced()): a cross-process Backpressure consumer claims a cursor and starts at the producer's CURRENT published seq, NOT 0, so the producer never blocks forever on a never-advancing cursor behind an overwritten slot. Lossless ONLY from attach point forward. Full lossless dynamic-attach handshake deferred to shimmy-uud.
acceptance_criteria: (1) Producer creates a named segment; a separate process opens it and reads messages. (2) Segment header versioned; opening with an incompatible layout fails with a clear error, never silent corruption. (3) Hugepage path works when available and is benchmarked with vs without. (4) Clean teardown/unlink; no leaked segments after a normal run. (5) Cross-process integration test passes.
notes:
FILES ADDED: include/shimmy/shm_segment.hpp (the whole layer), tests/shm_segment_test.cpp (8 tests). FILES MODIFIED: include/shimmy/ring.hpp (added Consumer move ctor [needed to return a Consumer by value from attach_consumer_at_current; moved-from relinquishes cursor_id_ so its dtor doesn't double-unregister] + Consumer::seek(seq) [cross-process attach primitive]); tests/CMakeLists.txt (added shm_segment_test.cpp to shimmy_tests, link rt on UNIX-not-APPLE, SHIMMY_TSAN_NO_FORK define gating the fork test out of the TSan build). NO change to CMakeLists.txt root, bench/, policies.hpp, wait_strategy.hpp, detail/cache.hpp.

VERIFIED inside nix develop: clang-18 26/26 ctest pass (was 18, +8 shm), clean build -Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic -Wconversion -Wshadow. gcc-13 26/26 pass, clean build. TSan (clang, SHIMMY_ENABLE_TSAN, BENCH=OFF) 25/25 pass (fork test gated out), 0 ThreadSanitizer warnings across 3 repeated runs. /dev/shm has 0 leaked shimmy segments after the run.

HUGEPAGE CAVEAT (verified-vs-assumed): host has HugePages_Total=0 and I lack privilege to reserve (vm.nr_hugepages is host-global). So the FALLBACK branch is RUNTIME-VERIFIED (test logs 'hugepages unavailable; cleanly fell back'); the MAP_HUGETLB ACTIVE branch is code-complete and compiles but was NOT runtime-exercised on this host. The active branch differs from fallback only in mmap flags (MAP_ANONYMOUS|MAP_HUGETLB) + 2MiB rounding. shimmy-7d8 should run the hugepage test on a host with reserved hugepages to confirm active-path + measure tail latency.

TSan + fork: the cross-process fork round-trip test is compiled out under TSan (SHIMMY_TSAN_NO_FORK) because TSan's runtime is not safe for fork-then-consume-over-shmem and cannot observe cross-process happens-before anyway; the shared Ring's race-freedom is already covered by shimmy-4d4's in-process TSan stress (identical bytes/atomics).

FOR shimmy-uud (discovery/handshake): build the real cross-process consumer registry on top of register_consumer()/attach_consumer_at_current(). Needs: (a) lossless-from-seq-0 cross-process Backpressure via an explicit start barrier in the header (a 'producer may start' gate + a consumer-count/epoch field), (b) crash-safety (dead consumer holding a cursor stalls a Backpressure producer forever — PID/heartbeat reclamation), (c) header already has a 'reserved' u32 + room to grow; bump SegmentHeader::kVersion when you change the layout. The state handshake (Initializing->Ready) is the seed of the gate.

FOR shimmy-7d8 (bench): ShmSegment<RingT>::create/open give you a cross-process channel; segment_bytes<RingT>() is the mapping size. Use opts.hugepages=true and check hugepages_active() to A/B hugepage tail latency (open question DESIGN §10). Header overhead is 128 bytes before the ring.
close_reason: Built the shared-memory segment lifecycle layer in include/shimmy/shm_segment.hpp (header-only). DECIDED/BUILT: (1) ShmSegment<RingT> RAII owner with create()/open() factories — create does shm_open(O_CREAT|O_TRUNC)+ftruncate+mmap+placement-new Ring(init())+release-fence+state=Ready; open does shm_open+fstat+mmap+full header validation+std::launder the live Ring (no re-init). (2) Versioned SegmentHeader (magic/version/blocksize/capacity/policy-id/maxconsumers + ring/slot struct-size fingerprints + Initializing->Ready handshake) validated field-by-field against the opener's OWN compile-time Ring; any mismatch throws shm_layout_error, never silently maps an incompatible layout. (3) Ring placed at round_up(sizeof(header),alignof(Ring)=64) computed identically both sides => address-independent, no pointers added to the shared region (Ring already pointer-free). (4) Unlink ownership: CREATOR owns shm_unlink (owns_name_); opener munmap+close only; verified no /dev/shm leak + ENOENT after creator dtor. (5) MAP_HUGETLB opt-in via anonymous MAP_SHARED|MAP_HUGETLB, graceful fallback when unavailable (never hard-fails), hugepages_active() reports which path. BACKPRESSURE CROSS-PROCESS STANCE: Overwrite fully supported; Backpressure lossless-from-0 NOT supported cross-process (cannot enforce the start-barrier across processes -> documented producer-deadlock hazard), instead attach_consumer_at_current() seeks a late consumer to the producer's current published seq (lossless from attach forward) to avoid the deadlock — full handshake deferred to shimmy-uud. Required minimal ring.hpp additions: Consumer move-ctor (return-by-value from the attach factory) + Consumer::seek(). VERIFICATION (all inside nix develop): clang-18 26/26 ctest pass clean (-Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic -Wconversion -Wshadow); gcc-13 26/26 pass clean; TSan(clang) 25/25 pass with 0 warnings x3 runs (fork test gated out via SHIMMY_TSAN_NO_FORK, justified). Criterion-3 hugepage benchmarking is shimmy-7d8's scope (path/fallback done; active branch not runtime-exercised here, no reserved hugepages/privilege). Not committed/pushed per instructions.

comments.author: Dustin
comments.text: HEADS-UP from shimmy-4d4 review: the Backpressure registration contract (all consumers must register before producer reaches Capacity) becomes much harder cross-process. In shared memory you cannot rely on an in-process start barrier — a process can mmap and start consuming at any time. shimmy-52e must either (a) restrict to Overwrite for the first cross-process cut, or (b) define how a Backpressure consumer safely attaches mid-stream (likely: start its cursor at the producer's current published seq, not 0). The general dynamic-attach/handshake solution is deferred to shimmy-uud. Decide and document which stance shimmy-52e takes. See ring.hpp register_consumer() comment for the full hazard description.

comments.author: Dustin
comments.text: Acceptance criterion (3) 'hugepage path benchmarked with vs without': the PATH is implemented, selectable (segment_options::hugepages), and its fallback is runtime-verified; the BENCHMARKING (tail-latency A/B) is explicitly the bench bead's responsibility (shimmy-7d8) per the implementation scope and DESIGN §10 open question — not benchmarked here by design. The hugepage ACTIVE branch was not runtime-exercised on this host (HugePages_Total=0, no privilege to reserve); fallback verified. All other criteria (1,2,4,5) fully verified across clang/gcc/TSan.

title: Collapse throughput_bench 4x run_cell+emit copy-paste into one helper
description: throughput_bench main() repeats 'auto cN = run_cell<B>(cc, messages, warmup, reps, producer_cpu, first_consumer_cpu, ncpu); emit(csv, cN, cfg);' four times for B in {64,256,1024,4096}, differing only in the block-size NTTP (plus the headline capture for 64B@1c). The NTTP genuinely can't be a runtime loop variable, but the repeated 7-arg threading + emit call is copy-paste that must be kept in lockstep when the cell signature or CSV schema changes. Collapse into a single run_and_emit<B>(...) helper invoked once per size.
acceptance_criteria: (1) One helper threads the shared run context (cc, messages, warmup, reps, cpus, ncpu, csv, cfg) so each size is one call. (2) Headline 64B@1-consumer capture preserved. (3) CSV output byte-identical to before for the same inputs. (4) Builds clang+gcc, bench runs.
close_reason: Collapsed the four run_cell<B>+emit copy-paste blocks into a single run_and_emit lambda taking a block-size integral_constant tag; the size sweep is now four one-line calls instead of four 3-line argument-threaded pairs. Headline 64B@1c capture preserved. CSV output verified byte-identical (16 rows, same {64,256,1024,4096}x{0,1,2,4} order + schema). Builds clang+gcc; throughput bench runs; all 28 tests pass.

title: shimmy: discovery, handshake & crash-safety (deferred)
description: Deferred future epic, NOT part of phase 1. Producer/consumer discovery and handshake (how a consumer finds a channel, registers a cursor, agrees on layout) plus crash-safety (dead-consumer detection via PID liveness/heartbeat, cursor reclamation when a consumer dies holding state). Also tracks further-out work: multi-NUMA, ARM64, multi-producer variants. Captured now so the knowledge isn't lost; do not start until phase-1 epic shimmy-t2d is complete.
design: Consumer registry lives in the shm header; cursor slot claim/release protocol; layout agreement against compile-time instantiation. Crash-safety: detect a dead consumer (PID liveness or heartbeat) and reclaim its cursor so Backpressure mode doesn't stall forever on a corpse. Multi-NUMA placement + cross-node coherence measurement, ARM64 (weak memory model, fence rigor, doubled test matrix), and MPMC/MPSC are separate later children of this epic.
acceptance_criteria: Not started in phase 1. When opened: (1) consumers discover + register against a running channel; (2) a dead consumer is detected and its cursor reclaimed without stalling the producer; (3) layout-mismatch on join fails loudly. Sub-scopes (multi-NUMA, ARM64, multi-producer) filed as children when this epic activates.

comments.author: Dustin
comments.text: Scope input from shimmy-4d4 review: the discovery/handshake protocol must solve safe LATE/DYNAMIC consumer attach for Backpressure. Current in-process ring assumes a fixed consumer set registered before the stream starts (enforced by a test-only start barrier). A dynamically-attaching Backpressure consumer that starts its cursor at 0 deadlocks the producer once the ring has lapped. The handshake should let a joining consumer agree on a safe starting sequence (e.g. producer's current published seq) and atomically register its cursor so the producer's await loop accounts for it without a window. Tie this to the crash-safety work (a dead consumer's cursor and a never-arriving consumer's cursor are the same stall from the producer's side).

value: shimmy's single-producer headline throughput (64B, 1 consumer, ~151M msgs/sec) is CACHE-COHERENCE BOUND, not frequency-bound. Proof: switching the host from powersave to performance CPU profile raised the 0-consumer drain ceiling ~41% (777M->1097M @64B) but left 64B/1-consumer FLAT (~151M). The bottleneck is the cache-line migrating from the producer's core to the reader's core when a freshly-published slot is read (Modified->Shared/Owned coherence transition), NOT CPU clock or per-message instruction count. Two regimes: small msgs (<=256B) coherence-bound; large msgs (>=1KB) memory-bandwidth-bound. ACTIONABLE: chasing clock speed or shaving instructions will NOT raise small-message throughput; only reducing coherence traffic (fewer/cheaper cache-line transitions, better line packing, amortizing the contended line) can. This is WHY batch-consume on Overwrite gave no throughput win in shimmy-68t (no contended cursor store to amortize on that path; the migrating slot line is the bottleneck). Any future throughput-speedup hypothesis must answer: does it reduce hot-path cache-line migrations? If not, it can't help small-message throughput. Documented in DESIGN.md section 5a.
